items.query: When was the EU AI Act officially published?
items.gt_answer: 12 July 2024

gt_supporting_facts.text: The EU AI Act was published in the Official Journal on 12 July 2024.

items.query: When did the EU AI Act enter into force?
items.gt_answer: 1 August 2024

gt_supporting_facts.text: The AI Act entered into force on 1 August 2024, twenty days after publication.

items.query: What are the four risk categories defined by the EU AI Act?
items.gt_answer: Unacceptable, high, limited, and minimal risk

gt_supporting_facts.text: The AI Act classifies systems into unacceptable, high, limited and minimal risk.

items.query: Which AI practices are prohibited under the EU AI Act?
items.gt_answer: Practices such as social scoring by public authorities and manipulative subliminal techniques

gt_supporting_facts.text: Prohibited practices include social scoring by public authorities.
Prohibited practices include manipulative subliminal techniques causing harm.

items.query: What obligations apply to high-risk AI systems?
items.gt_answer: Conformity assessment, risk management, data governance, and human oversight

gt_supporting_facts.text: High-risk AI systems must undergo conformity assessments before market placement.
High-risk systems require a risk management system throughout the lifecycle.
High-risk systems require human oversight measures.

items.query: What is the maximum administrative fine for prohibited AI practices?
items.gt_answer: Up to 35 million euros or 7% of global annual turnover

gt_supporting_facts.text: Fines for prohibited practices can reach 35 million euros or 7% of worldwide annual turnover.

items.query: Which body coordinates enforcement of the AI Act at EU level?
items.gt_answer: The European AI Office

gt_supporting_facts.text: The European AI Office coordinates enforcement and supervision at Union level.

items.query: How does the AI Act define a general-purpose AI model?
items.gt_answer: An AI model with significant generality able to perform a wide range of tasks

gt_supporting_facts.text: A general-purpose AI model displays significant generality and can perform a wide range of distinct tasks.

items.query: What transparency obligations apply to limited-risk AI like chatbots?
items.gt_answer: Users must be informed they are interacting with an AI system

gt_supporting_facts.text: Providers of limited-risk systems must ensure users are informed they interact with an AI system.

items.query: How does the EU AI Act relate to the GDPR on personal data?
items.gt_answer: The AI Act complements GDPR; data protection rules continue to apply

gt_supporting_facts.text: The AI Act applies without prejudice to the GDPR, which continues to govern personal data processing.
The GDPR establishes lawful bases for processing personal data.

items.query: What are the seven requirements for trustworthy AI in the EU ethics guidelines?
items.gt_answer: Human agency, technical robustness, privacy, transparency, diversity, societal wellbeing, accountability

gt_supporting_facts.text: Trustworthy AI guidelines list seven key requirements including human agency and oversight.
The requirements include technical robustness and safety.
The requirements include accountability.

items.query: Which annex lists high-risk AI use cases?
items.gt_answer: Annex III

gt_supporting_facts.text: Annex III enumerates the high-risk AI use cases such as biometric identification.

items.query: Is real-time remote biometric identification in public spaces banned?
items.gt_answer: It is prohibited in principle for law enforcement, with narrow exceptions

gt_supporting_facts.text: Real-time remote biometric identification in publicly accessible spaces for law enforcement is prohibited, subject to narrow exceptions.

items.query: What penalties apply for supplying incorrect information to authorities?
items.gt_answer: Up to 7.5 million euros or 1% of global annual turnover

gt_supporting_facts.text: Supplying incorrect, incomplete or misleading information can lead to fines up to 7.5 million euros or 1% of annual turnover.

items.query: What is a conformity assessment under the AI Act?
items.gt_answer: A procedure demonstrating a high-risk system meets the Act's requirements

gt_supporting_facts.text: A conformity assessment is the process demonstrating that a high-risk AI system complies with the requirements of the Act.

items.query: Which systems are considered minimal risk and largely unregulated?
items.gt_answer: Systems such as spam filters and AI-enabled video games

gt_supporting_facts.text: Minimal-risk systems, such as spam filters and AI in video games, face no additional obligations.

items.query: Compare obligations for high-risk versus limited-risk AI systems.
items.gt_answer: High-risk requires conformity assessment and oversight; limited-risk mainly requires transparency

gt_supporting_facts.text: High-risk systems require conformity assessment and human oversight.
Limited-risk systems primarily face transparency obligations.

items.query: When do obligations for general-purpose AI models start applying?
items.gt_answer: 12 months after entry into force (August 2025)

gt_supporting_facts.text: Obligations for general-purpose AI models apply from 12 months after entry into force.

items.query: What is the role of notified bodies?
items.gt_answer: Independent bodies that assess conformity of certain high-risk systems

gt_supporting_facts.text: Notified bodies are independent conformity assessment bodies designated by Member States.

items.query: Does the AI Act apply to providers established outside the EU?
items.gt_answer: Yes, if their AI system's output is used in the EU

gt_supporting_facts.text: The AI Act applies to providers outside the EU where the output of the system is used within the Union.

items.query: What documentation must providers of high-risk systems keep?
items.gt_answer: Technical documentation and automatically generated logs

gt_supporting_facts.text: Providers must draw up technical documentation before the system is placed on the market.
High-risk systems must enable automatic recording of logs over their lifetime.

items.query: How is 'serious incident' defined under the AI Act?
items.gt_answer: An incident leading to death, serious harm to health, or serious disruption of critical infrastructure

gt_supporting_facts.text: A serious incident is defined as an event leading to death or serious damage to health, property, or critical infrastructure.

items.query: What is the grace period before most AI Act rules fully apply?
items.gt_answer: 24 months after entry into force (August 2026)

gt_supporting_facts.text: Most provisions of the AI Act apply 24 months after entry into force.

items.query: Which regulator handles GDPR complaints about automated decisions?
items.gt_answer: National data protection authorities

gt_supporting_facts.text: National data protection authorities enforce the GDPR, including rules on automated individual decision-making.

items.query: What are codes of practice used for under the AI Act?
items.gt_answer: To detail compliance for general-purpose AI providers before harmonised standards exist

gt_supporting_facts.text: Codes of practice help general-purpose AI providers demonstrate compliance pending harmonised standards.

items.query: How many pages is the consolidated EU AI Act text?
items.gt_answer: Approximately 144 pages in the Official Journal

gt_supporting_facts.text: The consolidated AI Act text spans roughly 144 pages in the Official Journal.

items.query: Does the AI Act regulate open-source AI models?
items.gt_answer: Free and open-source models are partly exempt unless they are high-risk or GPAI with systemic risk

gt_supporting_facts.text: Free and open-source AI components benefit from exemptions unless they are high-risk or systemic-risk GPAI.

items.query: What threshold defines general-purpose AI with systemic risk?
items.gt_answer: Training compute above 10^25 floating-point operations

gt_supporting_facts.text: A general-purpose AI model is presumed to pose systemic risk when training compute exceeds 10^25 FLOP.

items.query: Who bears obligations when a system is substantially modified?
items.gt_answer: The party making the substantial modification is treated as a new provider

gt_supporting_facts.text: A party that substantially modifies a high-risk system assumes the obligations of a provider.

items.query: What is the sandbox mechanism in the AI Act?
items.gt_answer: Regulatory sandboxes let providers test innovative AI under supervision

gt_supporting_facts.text: AI regulatory sandboxes provide a controlled environment to develop and test AI systems under regulatory supervision.

items.query: What is the EU AI Act's position on faster-than-light communication?
items.gt_answer: Not addressed by the AI Act

items.query: What must deployers of emotion recognition systems do?
items.gt_answer: Inform natural persons exposed to the system

gt_supporting_facts.text: Deployers of emotion recognition systems must inform the natural persons exposed to them.
